title: 16_render_before_data.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: TypeError on First Paint

The profile card fetches `/api/users/:id` and renders the user's name and email. The first time the component mounts everything explodes: `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name')`. Hot-reload the page, the same error fires before the network request finishes.

The component declares `const [user, setUser] = useState();` (no initial value) and immediately reads `user.profile.name` in the JSX. React renders the component **once before any data has arrived** — `user` is `undefined`, and `undefined.profile` is the crash. The fetch fires from inside `useEffect`, which runs *after* the first render. By the time the data lands, the first render has already thrown.

This is the canonical render-before-data bug — every React data-flow lesson starts here. Fix it so the component handles its loading state before the fetch resolves.
traceback:
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'profile')
    at ProfileCard (profile-card.jsx:14)
    at renderWithHooks (react-dom.development.js:14985)
    at mountIndeterminateComponent (react-dom.development.js:17811)

The component renders ONCE before useEffect runs.
On that first render, user === undefined (no initial value passed to useState).
Reading user.profile crashes.
The fetch fires after this — too late, the first render has already thrown.
correctOutput:
PASS profile-card.test.jsx > renders skeleton then user Initial render:

Loading…

After fetch:

Ada Lovelace

ada@example.com

First render shows a loading placeholder; once the fetch resolves and setUser fires, React re-renders with the real data. No undefined-access crash.

options.label: Guard the render with `if (!user) return <Skeleton/>` so loading is an explicit state
options.resultLog:
PASS profile-card.test.jsx > renders skeleton then user Initial render:

Loading…

After fetch:

Ada Lovelace

ada@example.com
options.rationale: An **early return** on the loading state is the cleanest data-flow pattern in React. Before the data lands, `user` is `undefined` and the component returns the skeleton. After `setUser(data)` fires, React re-renders and the guard falls through to the real JSX. The component now has three explicit states — loading (no user), success (user present), error (not modelled here but added the same way: `const [error, setError] = useState()` and `if (error) return <ErrorBanner/>`). Every fetch component in any production codebase should follow this pattern; it's the foundation of the React data-flow lifecycle. Optional chaining (`user?.profile?.name`) silences the crash but renders empty content during loading, which looks like a bug to users; an explicit Skeleton or Loading message is what they expect.

options.label: Use optional chaining everywhere: `user?.profile?.name`
options.resultLog:
Crash is gone, but the first render shows:

For users this is a 'broken card' on every page load — an empty h2 and empty p with no indication that data is loading. Three frames later the real content pops in, which feels glitchy. Screen readers announce 'heading, paragraph' with no text. Optional chaining silences the bug; the loading state is still unhandled.
options.rationale: Optional chaining (`?.`) is a correct *syntactic* fix — `undefined?.profile` returns `undefined` without throwing — but it leaves the loading state visually unhandled. Users see an empty card for ~100-500ms on every page load, which is **worse** than a skeleton because there's no signal that data is incoming. Screen readers announce the structure with no text. Search engines (for SSR) index the page as empty. Optional chaining is appropriate when a value is *legitimately optional* (e.g. `user?.middleInitial`), not as a substitute for handling the loading state. The right fix is to model 'no data yet' as a first-class state with a deliberate UI.

options.label: Initialise `user` with `useState({ profile: { name: '', email: '' } })` so the empty values render harmlessly
options.resultLog:
Crash is gone, but the component cannot distinguish 'empty fake user' from 'real user
with empty name'. If the API returns a user whose name field happens to be empty, the
component silently shows the placeholder shape instead of an error.

Also, the placeholder shape must be kept in sync with the real API shape — every time
the backend adds a field, this default must grow too, or you get an undefined-access
crash when the JSX reads the new field.
options.rationale: A 'fake empty user' as the initial state silences the crash but introduces two new problems. (1) **State conflation**: the component can't distinguish 'data has not loaded yet' from 'data loaded but the user's name is empty', so the loading-state UI is impossible to add cleanly. (2) **Shape coupling**: the fake user has to mirror the real API's shape exactly, and stays in sync forever — every time the backend adds a field (e.g. `profile.avatar`), the placeholder must be updated too, or new code that reads `user.profile.avatar.url` will crash again. This pattern is sometimes called the **null object pattern**; it has a place (e.g. for collections, where `[]` is a clean empty value), but for richly-shaped objects it's the wrong abstraction. Model the loading state explicitly instead.

hints: What is the value of `user` on the very first render, before any fetch has finished? When does `useEffect` run relative to that first render?

Add `if (!user) return

Loading…

;` above the main JSX. React will render the loading state on first paint, then re-render with the real data once `setUser` fires inside the effect.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and you named the right principle (loading is an explicit state, not something to silence with optional chaining). The optional-chaining distractor is the most-common first attempt by juniors — it 'works' in the sense that the crash is gone, but the empty card on every page load is worse than the crash because nobody investigates it. The fake-user distractor (null object pattern) is the answer experienced developers sometimes reach for; it has legitimate uses but for component-level data it conflates two distinct states and creates ongoing schema-sync work. One follow-up: for components fetching the same resource often, consider extracting the loading/error/data state machine into a custom hook (`const { data, error, isLoading } = useUser(userId)`) — it deduplicates this pattern across the app and gives you one place to add cache invalidation, retries, and request cancellation. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## The React Data-Flow Lifecycle When a React component fetches data from an API, the order of operations is fixed and counter-intuitive on first encounter. Understanding it once saves dozens of debugging sessions later. ### What an API call is An **API call** is a network request from your browser to a server, asking for data. The server responds with structured data (usually JSON). In the browser, you make API calls with the `fetch` function or libraries like `axios`. The call is **asynchronous** — it returns a `Promise` immediately, and the data lands in your code only later, when the network response arrives. For a React component, this asynchrony has a specific consequence: **the component renders before the data exists**. ### The lifecycle, in order When a component mounts for the first time, React runs these steps: 1. **Hook initialisers run.** `useState()` returns `[undefined, setUser]` because no initial value was passed. `useState(null)` would return `[null, setUser]`. The value at this moment is whatever you supply (or `undefined`). 2. **The render function runs.** Your JSX is evaluated and React figures out what to put in the DOM. Anything that reads from state during this pass sees the **initial** value. 3. **React commits the DOM.** The browser shows what render produced. 4. **`useEffect` callbacks run.** This is where most fetches live. The fetch is sent. 5. **The network responds** — milliseconds to seconds later, depending on connection. 6. **`setUser(data)` queues a re-render.** React schedules another pass through render. 7. **Render runs again** — now with the real data — and the component shows the user's info. The crash happens at step 2, the *first* render. The fetch hasn't even started yet (step 4). `user` is `undefined`. Reading `user.profile.name` is reading `undefined.profile.name`, which throws. ### The fix: model the missing-data state The component needs to render *something* in step 2 even though there's no data yet. The cleanest pattern is an **early return**: ```jsx if (!user) return ; return ; ``` When `user` is `undefined`, the early return fires. When it's the real data, the early return is skipped and the main JSX renders. React's reconciliation handles the transition between the two states automatically. The pattern generalises to three states: ```jsx if (error) return ; // worst case: fetch failed if (!user) return ; // not yet: fetch in flight return ; // success: data is here ``` Each state has an explicit return. The render function tells the reader exactly what each state looks like. ### A worked example ```jsx import { useEffect, useState } from "react"; export default function ProfileCard({ userId }) { const [user, setUser] = useState(); // start undefined const [error, setError] = useState(); // start undefined useEffect(() => { let cancelled = false; fetch(`/api/users/${userId}`) .then(res => { if (!res.ok) throw new Error(res.statusText); return res.json(); }) .then(data => { if (!cancelled) setUser(data); }) .catch(err => { if (!cancelled) setError(err); }); return () => { cancelled = true; }; // cleanup if the component unmounts mid-flight }, [userId]); if (error) return

{error.message}

; if (!user) return

Loading…

; return (

{user.profile.name}

{user.profile.email}

); } ``` Read it top to bottom: state setup → effect that fetches → render that handles each state. Every junior React developer can write this pattern within their first month if shown it explicitly. ### Loading state as a first-class concept UI design treats a loading skeleton as a deliberate part of the user experience, not a quick afterthought. A good skeleton: - Matches the shape of the final content (a card-sized placeholder, not a tiny spinner). - Animates subtly (shimmer, pulse) so the user knows data is coming. - Appears within ~50ms of mount (the human reaction-time threshold). - Is announced as 'busy' to screen readers (`aria-busy="true"` on the container). The technical fix (early return) and the UX (skeleton design) are separate concerns; this lesson covers the technical fix. Real production components combine both. ### Common variations of this bug - `useState(null)` instead of `useState()`. Same behaviour: the early render still sees `null`, the `!user` check still fires. - `useState({})` to 'avoid undefined'. The crash moves: `user.profile.name` now reads `undefined.name` from `({}).profile`. Same bug, different line. - Using optional chaining (`user?.profile?.name`) — silences the crash but leaves the loading state UX broken. - Fetching outside `useEffect` — sometimes results in the fetch firing every render, which is a separate bug. - Returning `null` from the loading state — works but renders nothing, making the user think the page is blank. - Async fetches that resolve after the component unmounts — calls `setUser` on a dead component, React warns 'cannot update unmounted component'. Solved by the `cancelled` flag in the cleanup function above. ### You just learned React renders before the data arrives, so the component must explicitly model the state where data is missing. The early-return pattern (`if (!user) return ;`) is the canonical fix. Optional chaining silences the crash but leaves the UX broken. A null-object placeholder conflates two states and creates schema-sync work. Three explicit states — error / loading / success — three explicit returns. This is the foundation of every fetch component you'll ever write.
